text: The surgeon marks the incision site and proceeds with a vertical incision using a scalpel, cutting down to the fascia. S retractors are used to sweep the fat off the fascia at the midline. The surgeon carefully avoids catching the skin with the scalpel blade. The fascia is exposed and cut under direct vision, and the thin peritoneum is opened with the S-retractor. Two stay sutures are placed across the fascial opening, held with a hemostat. The S retractors are used to expose the fascia as the second stitch is placed. Additional sutures may be added at the end of the procedure.

The surgeon places an additional retractor inside the abdominal cavity, using it as a shoehorn. The Hassan Trocar's collar is adjusted to the appropriate length and locked in place, secured with stay sutures, and connected to insufflation tubing. The epigastric port is positioned high, just below the lower edge, directed towards the cystic duct, and slightly more lateral to avoid the falciform ligament. The table is turned to the patient's left without using reverse Trendelenburg. The surgeon positions their left hand close to the right, near the costal margin, ensuring the trocar is directed laterally to prevent close proximity inside. The lateral trocar is placed to minimize abdominal wall torquing, with a posterior to anterior trajectory, setting up the fundus grasper at an optimal angle.

The surgeon uses a five-millimeter, thirty-degree scope to visualize the surgical field. The gallbladder is retracted using a grasper inserted through the lateral trocar, demonstrating the angle needed to avoid puncturing the gallbladder. Adhesions between the gallbladder and liver capsule are cauterized and removed. The surgeon frees the duodenum from the gallbladder by applying traction, releasing adhesions around the gallbladder neck, and finding a suitable plane for dissection. The gallbladder is repositioned for better exposure, and the direction of retraction is adjusted to align with the cystic duct.

The surgeon visualizes the cystic artery and begins clearing fat from the gallbladder neck to better expose the cystic duct and artery. Using a hook, the surgeon carefully dissects the lateral peritoneal attachments and divides tissues with electrocautery. The gallbladder is freed, and the surgeon works on the medial aspect, using the hook to mobilize the cystic artery and create a window behind it. The surgeon adjusts the gallbladder position to improve exposure and enlarges the window using a grasper, aiming to define the anatomy clearly.

The surgeon continues to free the gallbladder by dividing lateral and medial peritoneal attachments using hook cautery. A small opening is made in the gallbladder, releasing bile, which is then suctioned. A 5mm clip applier is used to place clips on the cystic artery and gallbladder neck. The cystic duct is incised with scissors, and a 5 French open-ended ureteral catheter is inserted for an intraoperative cholangiogram, showing normal biliary tree opacification. The cystic duct is secured with three clips, and two additional clips are placed on the cystic artery.

The surgeon cuts the cystic duct and cystic artery, then proceeds to free the gallbladder from the gallbladder fossa. The left hand pulls the gallbladder laterally for better traction, while the hook cautery is used to dissect and expose a small vessel in the gallbladder fossa. The vessel is carefully clipped close to the liver. The gallbladder is flipped over the liver, and the gallbladder fossa is checked for hemostasis and bile leakage. The area is irrigated to ensure no bile leakage, and the gallbladder is completely released from the fossa.

The surgeon moves the laparoscopic camera to the epigastric trocar and places the gallbladder into a specimen bag. Using a suction irrigator, the abdomen is cleaned with small aliquots to remove bile. The gallbladder fossa is inspected for any remaining bile or bleeding before removing the upper trocars under visual guidance, concluding the procedure.
